text: The patient was placed in the right side up lateral position.
Ports were placed in the sixth, fourth, and second interspaces.
We used a combination of laparoscopic and conventional instrumentation as shown.
The parathyroid mass is seen next to the superior vena cava.
The phrenic nerve is easily seen in this view.
The azagus vein is demonstrated here.
The ultrasonic coagulator is used to open up the fibro fatty tissue.
This demonstrates the appearance of the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The nerve is carefully dissected away from the parathyroid using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection.
One of the challenges of this dissection is that the parathyroid tumor itself cannot be grasped.
It must be gently teased away from the surrounding tissue.
Part of the challenge in this case was obtaining sufficient traction and counter traction to dissect away the fibrous and fatty attachments to the parathyroid.
A significant portion of the tumor lay in a segment that was underneath the superior vena cava.
Thus, we used conventional instruments such as a ring clamp to elevate the cava, facilitating the dissection of the parathyroid out from underneath it.
The inferior parathyroids originate from the third pharyngeal pouch.
The third pharyngeal pouch differentiates in the fifth to sixth week of life.
The dorsal wing of this pouch becomes the parathyroid and the ventral wing of this pouch becomes the thymus.
These structures then migrate caudally
In normal development, the parathyroid separate from the thymus in about the seventh week of life, landing in the dorsal portion of the inferior pole of the thyroid.
The thymus continues into the anterior mediastinum.
In some cases,
the parathyroid continues to travel with the thymus on either the right or the left into the mediastinum.
Ectopic parathyroids may be found in as many as 20% of patients.
Failure of the parathyroid in development to release from the thymus may result in intrathoracic parathyroid as low as the aortopulmonary window
and the parathyroid may be found in either the anterior or posterior mediastinum.
There are case reports of giant mediastinal parathyroid adenoma in the literature.
Most of these lesions have been removed via thoracotomy, however.
Both the size of this lesion and the profound elevation of parathyroid hormone
are worrisome for the appearance of parathyroid carcinoma.
In this case, there was no evidence of metastasis or invasion into other structures.
As you can see at this portion, the final tongue of tissue underneath the superior vena cava is dissected free, allowing complete removal of the parathyroid and accompanying thymic tissue.
Here you can see the tumor was laying directly on top of the esophagus and trachea.
The recurrent laryngeal nerve is preserved and there is no evidence of bleeding.
The tumor is placed in an endo-catch bag and removed in its entirety.
The tumor measured approximately 8 centimeters in total length and represents one of the largest mediastinal parathyroids removed using a thoracoscopic technique.
Postoperative examination of the resection site is dry and a series of rib blocks were used to minimize postoperative discomfort.
The estimated blood loss was 15 cc's.
The case took 130 minutes.
The chest tube was removed on the first post-operative day and discharged on day three.
